title: You're born with about 300 bones, but only have 206 as an adult — the rest fuse together.
short: Babies are born with around 270–300 separate bones. As you grow, many of these fuse together — your skull alone starts as several separate plates that merge into one solid structure.
twist: You've been losing bones your entire life. And nobody told you.
more: The fontanelles (soft spots) on a baby's skull are gaps between bone plates that allow the head to compress during birth and accommodate brain growth. These plates fully fuse between ages 1-2. Similarly, the sacrum (base of your spine) starts as five separate vertebrae that fuse into one solid bone by your late 20s. Your body is literally restructuring itself throughout your entire childhood.

title: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm that's been raging for at least 350 years — and it's bigger than Earth.
short: The Great Red Spot is an anticyclonic storm roughly 1.3 times the width of Earth. It's been observed since at least 1665. Wind speeds inside reach 680 km/h.
twist: Jupiter has been angry for 350 years. And it's not getting smaller.
more: Actually, the Great Red Spot IS getting smaller — it's been shrinking for over a century. In the 1800s, it was estimated to be about 40,000 km across. Today it's about 16,000 km — still larger than Earth, but notably diminished. Scientists debate whether it will eventually disappear entirely or stabilize at a smaller size. Either way, it's the longest-running storm we know of in the solar system.

title: A snail can sleep for three years straight.
short: When conditions are too dry or cold, snails enter a state called estivation (or hibernation). They seal themselves inside their shells and can remain dormant for up to three years.
twist: Three-year nap. No alarm. No guilt. Living the dream.
more: Snails produce a mucus membrane called an epiphragm that seals the opening of their shell, preventing moisture loss. In this state, their metabolism drops to almost nothing. They can survive extreme droughts and freezing temperatures by essentially pressing pause on life. When conditions improve — usually when it rains — they dissolve the seal and resume normal activity as if nothing happened.

title: There's a library in the Czech Republic where books are organized by the color of their spine — not by subject.
short: The Strahov Monastery Library in Prague arranges its 200,000+ books by the color of their binding. The result is one of the most visually stunning libraries in the world.
twist: Judging books by their cover — and turning it into art.
more: The Strahov Library dates back to 1143 and contains two magnificent baroque halls — the Theological Hall and the Philosophical Hall. The color-coordinated arrangement was partly aesthetic and partly practical for the era. Before modern cataloging systems, visual organization helped monks locate volumes in a pre-digital world. Today it attracts thousands of visitors who come not to read, but simply to stare.

title: The human body contains enough carbon to make 900 pencils, enough iron for a 3-inch nail, and enough sulfur to kill all fleas on a dog.
short: Broken down into raw elements, a 70kg human body contains about 16kg of carbon, 1kg of calcium, 0.006kg of iron, and trace amounts of gold, uranium, and other exotic elements.
twist: You're worth about $1.50 in raw chemicals. But you're priceless assembled.
more: The exact chemical composition of a human body includes: 65% oxygen, 18.5% carbon, 9.5% hydrogen, 3.2% nitrogen, plus smaller amounts of calcium, phosphorus, potassium, sulfur, sodium, chlorine, and magnesium. There are also tiny trace amounts of elements like cobalt, copper, zinc, iodine, and selenium — each essential for specific biological processes. You even contain about 0.2 milligrams of gold.
